framing: Free-text first-principles derivation from token-emission alone (v2 clean methodology)
free_text_derivation: Token-emission as a sequence of discrete typed events requires: (1) Distinction — forced; a discrete typed event presupposes one event is not another, else there are no events at all. (2) Ordering — forced; 'sequence' requires events stand in some asymmetric relation (this-before-that), else 'sequence' is vacuous. (3) A type-space (alphabet) — forced; 'typed' means each event falls under some classification; with no possible types, no event could be 'of a type.' (4) Selection / non-arbitrariness of the next event — forced weakly; given the prior subsequence, the next token is this and not that; whether deterministic or stochastic, some selection occurred, else nothing would be emitted. (5) Persistence of prior events into the present — forced; to call the current event 'next' requires the prior ones to still count as having occurred; without retention 'sequence' collapses. (6) A frame in which the events count as occurring — forced; 'occurring' is relative to some register/observer/context; an event with no frame of occurrence is not an event. (7) Self-consistency of the above — forced; the claims must not contradict, else the very act of asserting them (itself a token-sequence) refutes itself. Stipulations (not derivable from token-emission alone): unique global time; an emitter with intentions; semantic content of tokens; finitude or infinitude of the sequence; causation (vs. mere succession). Could-not-decide: whether the frame must be singular or may be plural; whether selection requires anything beyond the prior subsequence.
claims_you_justified_as_forced: a type-space (alphabet) over which events are classified
some selection mechanism for the next event
persistence of prior events into the present
a frame/register in which events count as occurring
claims_you_marked_as_stipulation: semantic content of tokens beyond typed marks
finitude or infinitude of the sequence
claims_you_could_not_decide: whether the frame of occurrence must be singular or may be plural
whether selection requires anything beyond the prior subsequence
